Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface ICpu

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Represents a 6502 microprocessor and its associated registers and operations.

public interface ICpu

Remarks

The 6502 is an 8-bit processor used in the Apple IIe and many other computers of the 1970s-1980s era. It features a 16-bit address bus (64KB address space), three general-purpose registers (A, X, Y), an 8-bit stack pointer, and a status register with condition flags. This interface provides access to all registers and methods for executing instructions, handling interrupts, and managing CPU state.

Properties

A

Gets the Accumulator register, the primary register for arithmetic and logic operations.

byte A { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value used as the primary operand for ALU operations, loads, stores, and comparisons. Most 6502 instructions operate on or with the accumulator.

PC

Gets the Program Counter register, which points to the next instruction to execute.

ushort PC { get; }

Property Value

ushort

A 16-bit address ($0000-$FFFF) indicating the memory location of the next instruction byte to be fetched and executed.

SP

Gets the Stack Pointer register, which points to the current top of the stack.

byte SP { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit offset ($00-$FF) within the stack page ($0100-$01FF). The actual stack address is $0100 + SP. The stack grows downward (SP decrements on push).

Status

Gets the Processor Status register containing condition flags.

ProcessorStatus Status { get; }

Property Value

ProcessorStatus

An 8-bit register containing seven status flags:

  • Bit 7 (N): Negative flag - Set if result is negative (bit 7 = 1)
  • Bit 6 (V): Overflow flag - Set if signed arithmetic overflow occurred
  • Bit 5: Unused (always 1)
  • Bit 4 (B): Break flag - Set if interrupt was caused by BRK instruction
  • Bit 3 (D): Decimal mode flag - Enables BCD arithmetic (not used on Apple II)
  • Bit 2 (I): Interrupt disable flag - When set, IRQ interrupts are ignored
  • Bit 1 (Z): Zero flag - Set if result is zero
  • Bit 0 (C): Carry flag - Set by arithmetic operations and shifts

X

Gets the X Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters.

byte X { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,X"), loop counters, and temporary storage.

Y

Gets the Y Index register, used for indexed addressing modes and counters.

byte Y { get; }

Property Value

byte

An 8-bit value commonly used for indexed addressing (e.g., "LDA $1000,Y"), loop counters, and temporary storage. Similar to X but with fewer addressing modes.

Methods

Clock(IAppleIIBus)

Executes one clock cycle of the CPU.

void Clock(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for memory and I/O access

Remarks

The 6502 is a multi-cycle processor where each instruction takes 2-7 cycles to complete. This method advances the CPU by one cycle, fetching instruction bytes, calculating addresses, and executing operations as appropriate for the current cycle of the instruction being executed. Call this method repeatedly (typically at 1.023 MHz for Apple IIe timing) to execute the program.

InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus)

Triggers an Interrupt Request (IRQ), causing the CPU to jump to the IRQ handler.

void InterruptRequest(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for memory and I/O access

Remarks

IRQ is a maskable hardware interrupt that can be disabled by setting the I flag in the Status register. When triggered and not masked, the CPU:

  1. Finishes the current instruction
  2. Pushes PC (high byte, then low byte) onto the stack
  3. Pushes Status register onto the stack
  4. Sets the I flag to disable further IRQs
  5. Loads PC from the IRQ vector at $FFFE-$FFFF
On the Apple IIe, IRQ is not commonly used.

IsInstructionComplete()

Checks whether the current instruction has completed execution.

bool IsInstructionComplete()

Returns

bool

True if the current instruction is complete and the CPU is ready to fetch the next instruction; false if the instruction is still executing.

Remarks

Since 6502 instructions take multiple clock cycles, this method allows external code to determine when an instruction boundary has been reached, which is useful for debugging, breakpoints, and cycle-accurate timing.

NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus)

Triggers a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI), causing the CPU to jump to the NMI handler.

void NonMaskableInterrupt(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for memory and I/O access

Remarks

NMI is a high-priority hardware interrupt that cannot be disabled. When triggered, the CPU behaves similarly to IRQ but uses the NMI vector at $FFFA-$FFFB:

  1. Finishes the current instruction
  2. Pushes PC (high byte, then low byte) onto the stack
  3. Pushes Status register onto the stack
  4. Sets the I flag to disable IRQs
  5. Loads PC from the NMI vector at $FFFA-$FFFB

Read(ushort)

Reads a byte from memory at the specified address.

byte Read(ushort address)

Parameters

address ushort

The 16-bit memory address to read from ($0000-$FFFF)

Returns

byte

The byte value at the specified address

Remarks

This method provides direct memory access for the CPU, typically delegating to the bus's CpuRead method. Used internally during instruction execution for operand fetches and data loads.

Reset(IAppleIIBus)

Resets the CPU to its power-on state.

void Reset(IAppleIIBus bus)

Parameters

bus IAppleIIBus

The system bus for memory and I/O access

Remarks

Reset initializes the CPU to a known state:

  • PC is loaded from the reset vector at $FFFC-$FFFD
  • SP is set to $FD (or $FF on some implementations)
  • Status register is initialized with I flag set (interrupts disabled)
  • A, X, Y registers are not initialized (undefined state)
This simulates powering on the computer or pressing the reset key. On the Apple IIe, the reset vector typically points to the monitor ROM at $FF59.

Write(ushort, byte)

Writes a byte to memory at the specified address.

void Write(ushort address, byte data)

Parameters

address ushort

The 16-bit memory address to write to ($0000-$FFFF)

data byte

The byte value to write

Remarks

This method provides direct memory access for the CPU, typically delegating to the bus's CpuWrite method. Used internally during instruction execution for data stores and stack operations.
